g.data-narration: Distributed Mutual Exclusion ensures only one process can access shared resources at a time.
In distributed systems, multiple processes on different machines need to coordinate access to shared resources.
g.text: WHY DO WE NEED DISTRIBUTED MUTUAL EXCLUSION?
In distributed systems, multiple processes on different machines need to access
shared resources. Without coordination, simultaneous access leads to data corruption.
Unlike centralized systems, there's no shared memory or global clock!

g.data-narration: Race conditions occur when multiple processes read and write to shared data simultaneously causing inconsistency.
Three essential properties are safety, liveness, and fairness for any mutual exclusion algorithm.
Algorithms are classified into token-based and tokenless approaches based on message passing patterns.
Lamport's algorithm uses REQUEST, REPLY, and RELEASE messages for distributed mutual exclusion.
Lamport's algorithm requires 3(N-1) messages including REQUEST, REPLY, and RELEASE phases.
Ricart-Agrawala uses REQUEST and REPLY messages with deferred replies for efficiency.
Ricart-Agrawala requires only 2(N-1) messages as no RELEASE phase is needed.
Other notable algorithms include Maekawa's voting and Token Ring approaches with different trade-offs.
This example shows three processes with timestamps competing for critical section access.
This comparison shows message complexity for N=10 processes across different algorithms.
Remember these key points for exams: properties, message counts, and timestamp comparison logic.
g.text: 4. Timestamp comparison prevents deadlock (earlier = higher priority)

g.data-narration: Distributed mutex ensures coordinated access to critical sections with challenges like no shared memory.
g.text: - Distributed mutex ensures only ONE process accesses critical section
- Challenges: No shared memory, no global clock, unpredictable delays
- Token-based: Token circulates (Token Ring) | Tokenless: Lamport, Ricart

g.data-narration: Ricart-Agrawala is the most commonly tested algorithm with 2(N-1) message complexity.
g.text: - Lower messages not always better (trade-off: complexity, fault tolerance)

g.data-narration: Practice with timestamps and message flows to master distributed mutual exclusion concepts.
g.text: Practice with timestamps and message flows to master this topic!
